text: The surgeon uses a traumatic grasper to hold the fundus of the gallbladder and retracts it towards the diaphragm, providing better exposure for the procedure.
The surgeon uses a semi-traumatic Hartsman pouch grasper through the third port to hold the gallbladder.
The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel through the epigastric port to perform adhesiolysis, carefully separating adhesions around the gallbladder.
The surgeon uses the harmonic device through the epigastric port to slowly separate adhesions around the gallbladder, with the fundus retracted towards the diaphragm using a traumatic grasper.
The duodenum is visible in the surgical field, adjacent to the gallbladder being manipulated.
The surgeon employs a stripping technique to further separate tissue layers, enhancing the visibility of the gallbladder and surrounding structures.
The surgeon applies intramedial traction to the Hartman's pouch using a semi-traumatic grasper, aiding in the visualization and manipulation of the gallbladder during the cholecystectomy.
The surgeon separates the posterior peritoneum at the angle of the gallbladder body from the cystic duct, revealing the anatomical structures involved in the dissection.
The surgeon uses a harmonic device to create a knocking effect on the peritoneal blade, facilitating the separation of tissue layers.
The surgeon uses blunt dissection to create the posterior peritoneal dissection, separating tissue layers in the area around the gallbladder.
The surgeon uses a technique to facilitate the creation of a posterior window in the surgical field, likely involving manipulation of the gallbladder and surrounding tissues.
The surgeon positions the silicone jaw of the harmonic device towards the gallbladder wall to avoid puncturing the tissue during dissection.
The surgeon exposes the anterior peritoneum near the gallbladder, highlighting the tissue layers in the surgical field.
The surgeon applies anterolateral traction on the gallbladder fundus, occasionally rearranging the traction towards the right shoulder to facilitate anterior peritoneal dissection.

After removing the peritoneum and skeletonizing the cystic pedicle, the surgeon removes the harmonic device and introduces the Maryland dissector to further separate the peritoneum over the cystic duct.
The surgeon positions the Maryland dissector with its convexity oriented towards the liver, preparing for further dissection.
The surgeon identifies the common bile duct (CBD) within the surgical field.
The surgeon points out the common bile duct (CBD) in the surgical field.
The surgeon observes an indentation from anterior to posterior, using a surgical instrument to slowly open its jaws with a small initial opening.
The surgeon fully opens the jaw of the Maryland dissector to create a posterior window, with the convexity of the instrument oriented towards the liver.
The surgeon uses the left hand to apply anterolateral and anteromedial traction, while performing blunt dissection to create a window in the tissue.
The surgeon reveals a window between the cystic duct and cystic artery, exposing fibrous tissue in the area.

The surgeon carefully creates a window between the cystic duct and cystic artery, avoiding abrupt or tight opening of the Maryland dissector to prevent accidental damage to the artery or its branches.

The surgeon carefully opens the space between the cystic artery and cystic duct, defining the Calot's triangle. The liver, common hepatic duct, cystic artery, and cystic duct are visible. An anterior window is created, and the full length of the Maryland dissector, measuring 2.2 centimeters, is used to apply a clip.
The surgeon prepares to tie an extracorporeal Misra's knot around the cystic duct, ensuring a secure closure. The suture is fed through at least four times for enhanced security.
The surgeon fills the suture and pulls out the tail, ensuring there is no searing effect during the process.
The surgeon positions an instrument behind the cystic duct and artery to prevent searing effects while tying an extracorporeal knot outside the surgical field.
The surgeon demonstrates the technique of tying a knot by sequentially performing one hitch, one wind, and one lock, followed by additional hitches, winds, and locks to secure the suture.
The surgeon tightens the knot three times and introduces scissors through the epigastric port to cut the suture and its tail.
The surgeon completes the ligation of the cystic artery, which can be observed as securely tied off. Alternatively, the cystic artery could be clipped separately.
The surgeon uses a harmonic device to address the artery, opting not to tie a second knot due to the use of cholangiogram.
The surgeon uses a harmonic device to seal the part of the gallbladder, ensuring no leakage occurs from the sealed area.
The surgeon applies a titanium clip to the cystic artery, demonstrating its effectiveness in securing the vessel during the cholecystectomy procedure.
The surgeon applies a single titanium clip to the cystic artery, ensuring the tip of the clip is visible for proper placement.

The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to seal the gallbladder, complemented by the application of a titanium clip for enhanced security. The clip and harmonic work together to ensure effective sealing during the cholecystectomy.

The surgeon separates the remaining gallbladder from the liver by applying anteromedial and anterolateral traction, focusing on detaching the right and left peritoneal attachments before addressing the body of the gallbladder.

The surgeon maintains a good areolar plane while applying anteromedial traction, pushing the gallbladder neck towards the falciform ligament. Anterolateral traction is then applied as half of the gallbladder is removed. A 30-degree telescope is used with the light cable positioned downward to enhance visibility in the fossa, facilitating easier separation of the gallbladder from the liver.

The surgeon reaches the fundus of the gallbladder and removes the fundus grasper to prevent injury to the diaphragm. Using the left hand, the surgeon applies entrolateral and entromedial traction to continue separating the remaining gallbladder from the liver.
The surgeon carefully separates the gallbladder from the liver, ensuring it is not punctured to avoid the need for suction irrigation and potential spillage of stones.

The surgeon performs a final inspection of the gallbladder bed, checking for any bleeding or injury. The light cable is adjusted to enhance visibility, and the stump is examined to ensure it is secure. Once satisfied with the inspection, the surgeon completes the separation of the gallbladder from the liver.

The surgeon uses a 10 mm claw forceps to grasp the gallbladder at the level of the applied clip. The neck of the gallbladder is then hidden into the cannula, which is subsequently removed from the surgical field.
